title: Catching up and stagnating: Manufacturing wages relative to the US
subtitle: Unit 18 'The nation and the world economy’ Section 18.10 'Trade and growth' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at https://tinyco.re/18102330 [Figure 18.23]
caption:
Catching up and stagnating: Manufacturing wages relative to the US

Unit 18 'The nation and the world economy’ Section 18.10 'Trade and growth' in The CORE Team, The Economy. Available at https://tinyco.re/18102330 [Figure 18.23]

This line chart tracks manufacturing wages relative to the United States from 1950 to 2016, using a ratio scale where the US baseline is set at 100. The vertical axis utilizes a logarithmic scale with markers at 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100. Germany’s trajectory, represented by an orange line, begins near 25 in 1950, rises steadily to cross the 100 mark around 1980, and fluctuates between 100 and roughly 120 through 2016. Japan, shown in red, starts below 10 in the late 1950s, climbs sharply to surpass 100 by the mid-1990s, peaks above the US level, and declines to approximately 80 by the end of the period. South Korea’s blue line emerges near 8 in 1980 and ascends to roughly 60 by 2016. Mexico, in green, hovers between 10 and 20 for most of the timeline, starting near 25 in 1980 before dropping. China’s purple line begins near 2 in 1980, dips slightly, then rises to just under 10 by 2012. The Philippines and Sri Lanka remain in the lower bands, generally staying between 2 and 5. The United States line remains flat at the 100 level throughout the duration, serving as the reference point for all other nations.
